Numerical Linear Algebra

Least Squares via QR Factorization (Wrap-up)

Least Squares via QR Factorization (Wrap-up)

Recall from previous notes that solving the least squares problem min⁡∥Ax−b∥2\min \|Ax-b\|_2min∥Ax−b∥2​ using the Normal Equations (ATAx=ATbA^TAx = A^TbATAx=ATb) can be numerically unstable. Using the QR factorization (A=QRA = QRA=QR) provides a more stable approach.

Let A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}A∈Rm×n with m≥nm \ge nm≥n. Suppose AAA has full column rank nnn and admits a (thin) QR factorization

A=QR,Q∈Rm×n with Q⊤Q=In,R∈Rn×n upper triangular. A = Q R, \quad Q \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}\ \text{with } Q^\top Q = I_n,\quad R \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\ \text{upper triangular}. A=QR,Q∈Rm×n with Q⊤Q=In​,R∈Rn×n upper triangular.

Since QQQ is an orthogonal matrix, QTQ=IQ^TQ = IQTQ=I. The equation simplifies to: RTRx=RTQTbR^TRx = R^TQ^TbRTRx=RTQTb

Where RRR is an upper triangular matrix:

R=[r1,1…r1,n0⋱⋮0…rn,n0…0] R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{1,1} & \dots & r_{1,n} \\ 0 & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & \dots & r_{n,n} \\ 0 & \dots & 0 \end{bmatrix} R=​r1,1​000​…⋱……​r1,n​⋮rn,n​0​​

If all diagonal entries ri,i≠0r_{i,i} \neq 0ri,i​=0, then RRR is non-singular (invertible). We can multiply both sides of our equation by the inverse transpose, (RT)−1=R−T(R^T)^{-1} = R^{-T}(RT)−1=R−T:

R−TRTRx=R−TRTQTbIx=QTbRx=QTb \begin{gather} R^{-T}R^TRx = R^{-T}R^TQ^Tb \\ Ix = Q^Tb \\ Rx = Q^Tb \end{gather} R−TRTRx=R−TRTQTbIx=QTbRx=QTb​​

Because RRR is upper triangular, the system Rx=QTbRx = Q^TbRx=QTb can now be easily solved using backward substitution.

Floating Point Arithmetic

Computers cannot represent all real numbers precisely. They use a standardized floating-point representation:

fl(x)=±(d1.d2d3…dt)×βefl(x) = \pm (d_1 . d_2 d_3 \dots d_t) \times \beta^efl(x)=±(d1​.d2​d3​…dt​)×βe

Components:

  • Mantissa (Significand): d1.d2…dtd_1 . d_2 \dots d_td1​.d2​…dt​ (determines precision)
  • Base: β\betaβ (usually 2 for binary)
  • Exponent: eee (determines range)

Due to rounding and truncation, standard algebraic rules do not always hold in computational arithmetic:

  • fl(x+y)≠x+yfl(x + y) \neq x + yfl(x+y)=x+y
  • Associativity is lost: fl(fl(x+y)+z)≠fl(x+fl(y+z))fl(fl(x+y)+z) \neq fl(x+fl(y+z))fl(fl(x+y)+z)=fl(x+fl(y+z))

Example: Catastrophic Cancellation (Swamping)

Consider the system of equations:

−10−4x+y=1x+y=2 \begin{array}{ccc} -10^{-4}x + y & = & 1 \\ x + y & = & 2 \end{array} −10−4x+yx+y​==​12​

Paper Calculation (Exact):

[−10−411112]→R2=R2−(−104)R1[−10−41101000110002] \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} -10^{-4} & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \end{array} \right] \xrightarrow{R_2 = R_2 - (-10^4)R_1} \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} -10^{-4} & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 10001 & 10002 \end{array} \right] [−10−41​11​12​]R2​=R2​−(−104)R1​​[−10−40​110001​110002​]

This gives y≈1y \approx 1y≈1 and x≈1x \approx 1x≈1.

Computer Calculation (Truncated): If a computer can only hold 3 significant digits, adding 10410^4104 and 111 simply yields 10410^4104.

[−10−41101000020000] \left[ \begin{array}{cc|c} -10^{-4} & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 10000 & 20000 \end{array} \right] [−10−40​110000​120000​]

Solving this yields y=2y = 2y=2 and x=10000x = 10000x=10000. The small error in yyy completely blew up the calculation for xxx.

How do we fix this?

Adjust the pivots! This is why we use Partial Pivoting in Gaussian Elimination. Always swap rows so the largest absolute value in the column is used as the pivot, preventing tiny numbers from blowing up the arithmetic.

Vector Norms

A norm is a function ∥⋅∥:Rn→R\|\cdot\| : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}∥⋅∥:Rn→R that gives a strictly positive “length” or “size” to a vector. To be a valid norm, it must satisfy three properties for all vectors x,yx,yx,y and scalars α\alphaα:

  1. Positive Definiteness: ∥x∥≥0\|x\| \ge 0∥x∥≥0, and ∥x∥=0  ⟺  x=0\|x\| = 0 \iff x = 0∥x∥=0⟺x=0
  2. Absolute Homogeneity: ∥αx∥=∣α∣∥x∥\|\alpha x\| = |\alpha|\|x\|∥αx∥=∣α∣∥x∥
  3. Triangle Inequality: ∥x+y∥≤∥x∥+∥y∥\|x+y\| \le \|x\| + \|y\|∥x+y∥≤∥x∥+∥y∥

Common Vector Norms:

∥x∥1=∑i=1n∣xi∣(Manhattan / ℓ1 norm)∥x∥2=(∑i=1n∣xi∣2)1/2=x⊤x(Euclidean / ℓ2 norm)∥x∥∞=max⁡1≤i≤n∣xi∣(Maximum / ℓ∞ norm)∥x∥p=(∑i=1n∣xi∣p)1/p(General ℓp norm, p≥1) \begin{align} \|x\|_1 & = \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i| & & \text{(Manhattan / } \ell_1 \text{ norm)} \\ \|x\|_2 & = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i|^2\right)^{1/2} = \sqrt{x^\top x} & & \text{(Euclidean / } \ell_2 \text{ norm)} \\ \|x\|_\infty & = \max_{1 \le i \le n} |x_i| & & \text{(Maximum / } \ell_\infty \text{ norm)} \\ \|x\|_p & = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i|^p\right)^{1/p} & & \text{(General } \ell_p \text{ norm, } p \ge 1\text{)} \end{align} ∥x∥1​∥x∥2​∥x∥∞​∥x∥p​​=i=1∑n​∣xi​∣=(i=1∑n​∣xi​∣2)1/2=x⊤x​=1≤i≤nmax​∣xi​∣=(i=1∑n​∣xi​∣p)1/p​​(Manhattan / ℓ1​ norm)(Euclidean / ℓ2​ norm)(Maximum / ℓ∞​ norm)(General ℓp​ norm, p≥1)​​

Matrix Norms

Matrix norms share the same core properties as vector norms, with an additional property for matrix multiplication:

  • Submultiplicativity: ∥AB∥≤∥A∥∥B∥\|AB\| \le \|A\|\|B\|∥AB∥≤∥A∥∥B∥

Induced Matrix Norms

An induced matrix norm represents the maximum amount a matrix AAA can “stretch” a vector xxx: ∥A∥p=sup⁡x≠0∥Ax∥p∥x∥p\|A\|_p = \sup_{x \neq 0} \frac{\|Ax\|_p}{\|x\|_p}∥A∥p​=supx=0​∥x∥p​∥Ax∥p​​

  • 1-Norm (Maximum Absolute Column Sum): ∥A∥1=max⁡1≤j≤n(∑i=1m∣aij∣)\|A\|_1 = \max_{1 \le j \le n} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{m} |a_{ij}| \right)∥A∥1​=max1≤j≤n​(∑i=1m​∣aij​∣)
  • ∞\infty∞-Norm (Maximum Absolute Row Sum): ∥A∥∞=max⁡1≤i≤m(∑j=1n∣aij∣)\|A\|_\infty = \max_{1 \le i \le m} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} |a_{ij}| \right)∥A∥∞​=max1≤i≤m​(∑j=1n​∣aij​∣)

Frobenius Norm (Non-Induced)

The Frobenius norm treats the matrix like one giant vector. It is computationally easier to calculate than the induced 2-norm.

∥A∥F=(∑i=1m∑j=1n∣aij∣2)12 \|A\|_F = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} |a_{ij}|^2 \right)^{\frac{1}{2}} ∥A∥F​=(i=1∑m​j=1∑n​∣aij​∣2)21​

It can also be written using the trace operation (the sum of the diagonal elements): ∥A∥F=tr(ATA)=tr(AAT)\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\text{tr}(A^TA)} = \sqrt{\text{tr}(AA^T)}∥A∥F​=tr(ATA)​=tr(AAT)​

Takeaway
  • Avoid large multipliers in elimination: use (partial) pivoting.
  • Large intermediate numbers amplify rounding and truncation errors.
  • QR-based methods are often more stable for least-squares than forming normal equations A⊤AA^\top AA⊤A explicitly. =======

Condition Number of a Matrix

The condition number, κ(A)\kappa(A)κ(A), measures how sensitive the solution of a linear system Ax=bAx=bAx=b is to small changes (errors) in the data bbb. In other words, it measures how close a non-singular matrix is to being singular (having columns that are “almost” linearly dependent).

κ(A)=∥A∥⋅∥A−1∥\kappa(A) = \|A\| \cdot \|A^{-1}\|κ(A)=∥A∥⋅∥A−1∥

Key Observations:

  1. Identity Matrix: κ(I)=1\kappa(I) = 1κ(I)=1 (Perfectly conditioned).
  2. Lower Bound: κ(A)≥1\kappa(A) \ge 1κ(A)≥1 for any matrix.

Proof that κ(A)≥1\kappa(A) \ge 1κ(A)≥1: Using the submultiplicative property of matrix norms:

1=∥I∥=∥AA−1∥∥AA−1∥≤∥A∥∥A−1∥1≤∥A∥∥A−1∥  ⟹  1≤κ(A) \begin{gather} 1 = \|I\| = \|A A^{-1}\| \\ \|A A^{-1}\| \le \|A\| \|A^{-1}\| \\ 1 \le \|A\| \|A^{-1}\| \implies 1 \le \kappa(A) \end{gather} 1=∥I∥=∥AA−1∥∥AA−1∥≤∥A∥∥A−1∥1≤∥A∥∥A−1∥⟹1≤κ(A)​​
Remark

If κ(A)\kappa(A)κ(A) is close to 111, the matrix is well-conditioned (stable). If κ(A)\kappa(A)κ(A) is very large, the matrix is ill-conditioned, meaning its rows/columns are nearly linearly dependent, and floating-point errors will be heavily magnified during computation.
